issuesFound.title: Missing label for form input
issuesFound.explanation: Several form inputs lack proper labels. Screen readers will announce them as "text field" or "input" without context, making them inaccessible. This violates WCAG 2.1.1 (Name, Label, Value).
issuesFound.fix:
Add `<label>` elements for each input. Example fixes:
  Line 130: `<input type="email" id="newsletter-email" name="email" autocomplete="email" required aria-required="true" placeholder="you@example.com">` → `<label for="newsletter-email">Email address</label><input type="email" id="newsletter-email" name="email" autocomplete="email" required aria-required="true" placeholder="you@example.com">`
  Line 131: `<input type="checkbox" id="newsletter-consent" name="consent" required aria-required="true">` → `<label for="newsletter-consent">I agree to receive marketing emails</label><input type="checkbox" id="newsletter-consent" name="consent" required aria-required="true">`
  Line 132: `<input type="text" id="contact-first-name" name="first_name" required>` → `<label for="contact-first-name">First name</label><input type="text" id="contact-first-name" name="first_name" required>`
  Line 133: `<input type="text" id="contact-last-name" name="last_name" autocomplete="family-name" required aria-required="true">` → `<label for="contact-last-name">Last name</label><input type="text" id="contact-last-name" name="last_name" autocomplete="family-name" required aria-required="true">`
  Line 134: `<input type="email" id="contact-email" name="email" autocomplete="work email" required aria-required="true">` → `<label for="contact-email">Email address</label><input type="email" id="contact-email" name="email" autocomplete="work email" required aria-required="true">`
  Line 135: `<input type="text" id="contact-company" name="company" autocomplete="organization">` → `<label for="contact-company">Company name</label><input type="text" id="contact-company" name="company" autocomplete="organization">`

issuesFound.title: Missing aria-labelledby for nav element
issuesFound.explanation: Multiple `<nav>` elements lack distinguishing labels. Screen readers will announce them as "navigation" without context, making it difficult to distinguish between multiple navigation sections. This violates WCAG 1.3.1 (Information and Relationships) and ARIA best practices.
issuesFound.fix:
Add `aria-labelledby` to each `<nav>` element. Example fixes:
  Line 36: `<nav id="main-nav">` → Add `aria-labelledby="main-nav-label"` or reference a nearby heading.
  Line 106: `<nav aria-label="Product category filter">` → `<nav aria-labelledby="filter-label">` (already correct)

  Line 116: `<nav aria-label="Company navigation">` → `<nav aria-labelledby="company-heading">` (already correct)
  Line 121: `<nav aria-label="Legal navigation">` → `<nav aria-labelledby="legal-heading">` (already correct)

  Since the `<nav>` elements in the footer are already labeled with `aria-label` or `aria-labelledby` via their parent sections, the primary issue is the `<nav>` in the account/cta section (line 55) and the filter tabs (line 106). Correcting the main navigation and ensuring consistent labeling:

  Line 55: `<nav>` (no label) → Add `aria-labelledby="account-nav-label"` or reference a heading nearby.

  For the filter tabs, ensure consistent labeling:
  Line 106: `<nav role="group">` → `<nav aria-labelledby="filter-label">` (already correct, but ensure the label is properly associated with the nav)

  The primary `<nav>` in the header (line 36) should be labeled:
  Line 36: `<nav id="main-nav">` → Add `aria-labelledby="header-nav-label"`

  Line 106: `<nav role="group">` → `<nav aria-labelledby="filter-label">` (already correct)

issuesFound.title: Heading level skip
issuesFound.explanation: There is a heading level skip from h1 (line 22, id="hero-heading") to h2 (line 26, id="features-heading") with no intervening h2. This affects screen reader users who rely on heading structure to navigate content, as the sudden jump may cause confusion about content hierarchy.
issuesFound.fix:
Line 22:

Build accessible products — faster

Line 26:

Everything you need to ship accessible software

(No code change needed if the heading structure is intentional, but to fix the skip, either add an h2 between them or change the h2 to h3. However, since the h2 is a section heading, it is likely correct. Upon review: h1 → h2 is a valid sequence (no skip). A skip is defined as a jump of 2 or more levels (e.g., h1 to h3). h1 to h2 is a jump of 1 level, which is allowed. Therefore, no heading skip exists. No issue is reported.)
